questions.text: What happens when a container exceeds its memory limit in Kubernetes?

options.text: It is throttled like CPU
It is OOMKilled (Out Of Memory killed) by the kernel
The Pod is rescheduled to a node with more memory
The container is paused until memory is available

questions.explanation: When a container exceeds its memory limit, the Linux kernel's OOM killer terminates it. Kubernetes then restarts the container based on the Pod's restartPolicy. This is why it's critical to set appropriate memory limits and requests. CPU, by contrast, is throttled rather than killed.

questions.text: What does a readinessProbe failure cause?

options.text: The container is restarted
The Pod is removed from Service endpoints (no traffic sent)
The Pod is evicted from the node
The container image is re-pulled

questions.explanation: A failed readinessProbe removes the Pod from Service endpoint lists — traffic stops being sent to it. The container is NOT restarted (that's what livenessProbe failure does). This allows Pods to indicate they're temporarily unable to serve traffic during startup, config reload, or maintenance.

citations.label: Configure liveness, readiness and startup probes

questions.text: What is the purpose of a startupProbe?

options.text: Verifies the container image downloads successfully
Delays liveness/readiness probes for slow-starting applications
Runs health checks before Pod scheduling
Replaces both liveness and readiness probes

questions.explanation: startupProbe protects slow-starting containers from being killed by livenessProbe before they finish initializing. The liveness and readiness probes are disabled until the startupProbe succeeds. Once successful, startupProbe stops and liveness/readiness take over.

questions.text: What unit does Kubernetes use for CPU resource requests?

options.text: Percentage (e.g., 50%)
CPU cores (e.g., 0.5) or millicores (e.g., 500m)
MHz (e.g., 1000MHz)
Threads (e.g., 2 threads)

questions.explanation: CPU resources are expressed in cores (1 = 1 full vCPU) or millicores (1000m = 1 core, 250m = 0.25 vCPU). Resource requests affect scheduling (the scheduler finds nodes with enough free capacity) while limits control the container's CPU usage via cgroups.

questions.text: What are init containers used for?

options.text: Running continuously alongside app containers
Running setup tasks to completion before app containers start
Initializing Kubernetes cluster components
Providing shared binaries to app containers

questions.explanation: Init containers run sequentially before app containers start, and must complete successfully. Use cases: waiting for a service to be ready, running database migrations, populating configuration files, fetching secrets from a vault. If an init container fails, the Pod is restarted per restartPolicy.

questions.text: What is the difference between resource 'requests' and 'limits'?

options.text: Requests are for CPU only; limits are for memory only
Requests are what the scheduler reserves; limits are the maximum the container can use
Requests are minimums enforced at runtime; limits are scheduling hints
They are identical — one is deprecated

questions.explanation: Requests are what the scheduler uses to find a node with sufficient capacity — the node must have at least this much free. Limits are enforced at runtime via cgroups — the container cannot exceed them. A container can use more than its request (up to the limit) if the node has spare capacity.

questions.text: Which probe type checks if a TCP connection can be established?

options.text: httpGet
tcpSocket
exec
grpc

questions.explanation: tcpSocket probes attempt to open a TCP connection to a specified port. If the connection can be established, the probe succeeds. This is useful for non-HTTP services like databases or message queues. httpGet is for HTTP services; exec runs a command; grpc uses the gRPC health protocol.

questions.text: What is a 'sidecar container' in a Pod?

options.text: A container that runs before main containers (like init)
A secondary container running alongside the main container providing supporting functionality
A container template stored separately
A container running outside the Pod for monitoring

questions.explanation: Sidecar containers run alongside the main application container in the same Pod, sharing network and storage. Common examples: log shippers (Fluentd), service mesh proxies (Envoy/Istio), secret injectors, or TLS terminators. Kubernetes 1.29+ has native sidecar container support with the sidecarContainers API.

options.text: Shows the last 10 log lines
Streams logs in real-time (follows log output)
Filters logs for errors only
Shows logs from all Pods with the same label

questions.explanation: kubectl logs -f (follow) streams log output from a container in real-time, similar to 'tail -f'. Use -c <container-name> for multi-container Pods. Use --previous to see logs from the last terminated container instance (useful for debugging crash-loops).

questions.text: What happens to a Pod with restartPolicy: Never when its container exits?

options.text: The container is restarted once then deleted
The Pod enters Completed or Failed status and is never restarted
The Pod is rescheduled on a different node
The container is replaced with a new one from a fresh image pull

questions.explanation: With restartPolicy: Never, if the container exits (success or failure), the Pod moves to Completed or Failed state and the container is not restarted. This is appropriate for batch Jobs. restartPolicy: OnFailure restarts only on non-zero exit; Always (default) always restarts.
